subtitle: The Rice Bearer - God of Prosperity, Foxes, and Abundance
name: Japanese - Inari

listDisplay.primary: Japanese - Inari - The Rice Bearer - God of Prosperity, Foxes, and Abundance

gridDisplay.title: Japanese - Inari
gridDisplay.subtitle: The Rice Bearer - God of Prosperity, Foxes, and Abundance

search.searchableText: japanese - inari the rice bearer - god of prosperity, foxes, and abundance japanese - inari japanese inari

icon:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 64 64">
  <circle cx="32" cy="32" r="28.8" fill="#8b7fff" opacity="0.2"/>
  <path d="M 32 12.8 L 44.8 51.2 L 19.2 51.2 Z" fill="#8b7fff"/>
  <circle cx="32" cy="32" r="9.6" fill="#9370DB"/>
  <text x="32" y="57.6" text-anchor="middle" font-size="19.2" fill="#8b7fff">⚡</text>
</svg>
description:
Inari, often referred to as Japanese - Inari, is a prominent and multifaceted deity deeply embedded within the Shinto religious landscape of Japan. Primarily venerated as the *kami* (god or spirit) of rice, Inari's dominion extends considerably beyond simple agriculture, encompassing prosperity, fertility, commerce, tea, sake, general household well-being and, perhaps most famously, foxes. This diverse portfolio has solidified Inari's position as one of the most widely worshipped deities in Japan, evidenced by the ubiquity of Inari shrines throughout the country, easily recognized by the characteristic presence of fox statues (kitsune). The precise genesis of Inari worship remains somewhat obscure, with different regions and traditions offering varying, and sometimes conflicting, accounts. However, the deity's unwavering association with rice cultivation consistently forms a central, unifying theme.

The earliest documented instances of Inari worship date back to the Nara period (710-794 CE), with the founding of the Fushimi Inari Shrine in 711 CE widely considered a pivotal event in the deity's history. The establishment of this shrine is intrinsically linked to the Hata clan, a powerful immigrant group who exerted considerable influence in agricultural development and silk production. Legend recounts how a member of the Hata clan discharged an arrow into a rice cake, which subsequently transformed into a white fox and ascended to the summit of Mount Inari, thereby designating the site as sacred. This narrative underscores the interconnected relationship between Inari, rice, and the fox, a relationship that would become a defining characteristic of Inari's iconography and religious practice. The association with the Hata clan also provides a potential historical grounding for the deity's rise to prominence, connecting it to tangible socio-economic forces within early Japanese society.

The name "Inari" itself is etymologically linked to "ine nari" (稲成り), which translates to "rice growing" or "becoming rice," directly reflecting the deity's primary association with the cultivation of this essential staple crop. However, it is crucial to recognize that Inari's identity is not monolithic; it is not confined to a single, immutable form. Inari can manifest in a variety of guises, sometimes depicted as a male figure, sometimes as a female figure, and frequently as an androgynous or collective entity. This fluidity in representation reflects the syncretic nature of Japanese religious beliefs and the adaptability of the deity to diverse regional and cultural contexts. One prevalent manifestation is Ukanomitama (宇迦之御魂神), a deity mentioned in both the *Kojiki* (Records of Ancient Matters) and the *Nihon Shoki* (Chronicles of Japan), two of the oldest and most authoritative texts of Japanese history and mythology. Ukanomitama is explicitly associated with grains and food, thereby further cementing Inari's connection to agricultural abundance and sustenance. Another name closely associated with Inari is Ōgetsuhime-no-kami (大宜都比売神), a goddess who, according to myth, was tragically killed, and from whose deceased body various grains and crops miraculously sprang forth. This association links Inari to the cyclical processes of death and rebirth, and to the generative power inherent within the earth itself. This connection to Ōgetsuhime-no-kami highlights the broader themes of sacrifice and the transformation of death into life that are sometimes associated with Inari worship.

The fox, or *kitsune* (狐), plays an indispensable and multifaceted role in Inari worship, functioning as Inari's messengers, attendants, and, in some interpretations, even as manifestations of the deity's power. These foxes are not typically considered to be the deity *per se*, but rather intermediaries who facilitate communication and interaction between the human and divine realms. Fox statues, frequently adorned with red bibs (a practice influenced by Buddhist traditions), are strategically positioned to guard the entrances to Inari shrines, and are routinely offered gifts of rice, sake, and other delicacies as a form of veneration. The association between Inari and foxes is complex and layered. Foxes are renowned for their intelligence, cunning, and perceived ability to protect crops from rodents and other agricultural pests. This practical benefit likely contributed significantly to their elevated status within Inari's cult. Furthermore, foxes were traditionally believed to possess supernatural powers, including shapeshifting, illusion-casting, and divination, further enhancing their mystique and solidifying their connection to the divine. According to some folklore, foxes can gain additional powers as they age, growing additional tails and becoming increasingly powerful spirits.

While foxes are almost universally associated with Inari, it is crucial to acknowledge that not all foxes are considered inherently benevolent. In Japanese folklore, a distinction is often drawn between *zenko* (善狐, good foxes) and *yako* (野狐, field foxes), with the latter often associated with trickery, deception, and malevolent intentions. However, the foxes that serve Inari are generally regarded as *zenko*, benevolent and protective spirits who act in the best interests of those who venerate the deity. The red bibs worn by the fox statues are also believed to have protective qualities, warding off negative influences and ensuring the well-being of those who approach the shrine.

Inari shrines are characteristically adorned with vibrant red *torii* (鳥居) gates, creating a visually striking and spiritually charged atmosphere. The color red is traditionally believed to possess apotropaic qualities, capable of warding off evil spirits and attracting good fortune. It is also associated with vitality, energy, and the life-giving power of the sun. The practice of donating *torii* gates to Inari shrines has evolved into a widespread tradition, culminating in the iconic tunnels of vermillion gates found at Fushimi Inari and other major shrines. These tunnels are not merely decorative embellishments; they represent a symbolic pathway through the sacred space, inviting pilgrims to connect with the divine presence of Inari and to embark on a transformative spiritual journey. Walking through these tunnels is considered an act of purification and a means of drawing closer to the deity.

Inari's influence transcends the boundaries of agriculture and commerce. The deity is also associated with household safety, success in business ventures, and the fulfillment of personal wishes. Consequently, Inari shrines are frequented by individuals from all strata of society, seeking blessings for a wide spectrum of life's endeavors. Business owners often offer prayers to Inari for prosperity and commercial success, while families may seek protection from harm and the bestowal of good fortune upon their households. This broad appeal has cemented Inari's position as one of the most popular and beloved deities in Japan, a testament to the deity's ability to resonate with the diverse needs and aspirations of the Japanese people.

The syncretic nature of Japanese religious practices has also facilitated the integration of Inari into Buddhist traditions. In some Buddhist temples, Inari is revered as Dakiniten (荼枳尼天), a goddess of wealth, prosperity, and spiritual power. Dakiniten is often depicted riding a white fox, further reinforcing the connection between Inari and the fox messenger. This blending of Shinto and Buddhist beliefs exemplifies the fluid and adaptable character of Japanese religious syncretism, where deities and concepts from different traditions are often assimilated and reinterpreted within a new context.

The rituals associated with Inari worship are diverse and vary depending on the specific shrine, regional customs, and individual practices. Offerings of rice, sake, tofu, and other foods are commonly made to Inari and the fox messengers as a sign of respect and gratitude. Prayers are recited, and petitions are submitted, requesting blessings, guidance, and the fulfillment of specific wishes. Festivals dedicated to Inari are celebrated throughout the year, often featuring processions, dances, theatrical performances, and other festive activities that serve to honor the deity and to strengthen the community's connection to Inari's divine power.

One significant aspect of Inari worship revolves around the concept of *tama* (魂), which refers to the spirit, soul, or essence of a thing. In the context of Inari, *tama* represents the life force inherent in rice and other crops, as well as the vital energy that sustains prosperity and well-being. Offerings to Inari are intended to nourish and strengthen the *tama*, ensuring continued abundance and fertility. The deity Miketsu no Kami (御饌津神) is also closely associated with Inari, and its name literally translates to "god of offerings," underscoring the paramount importance of ritual offerings in Inari worship.

In contemporary Japan, Inari remains a vibrant and relevant deity, deeply interwoven into the fabric of modern life. Inari shrines continue to flourish, attracting millions of visitors annually. The deity's enduring association with commerce and prosperity has made Inari particularly popular among business owners, entrepreneurs, and those seeking financial success. Furthermore, the image of the fox remains a potent symbol of good fortune and is frequently employed in advertising, popular culture, and various forms of artistic expression. The continued popularity of Inari reflects the deep-seated connection between the Japanese people and their agricultural heritage, as well as the enduring appeal of a deity who embodies prosperity, abundance, and the transformative power of nature. Inari's remarkable adaptability and seamless integration into diverse aspects of Japanese life have ensured the deity's continued relevance in a rapidly changing world. From the ancient rice fields to the bustling urban centers, Inari's pervasive presence continues to be felt throughout Japan, serving as a testament to the enduring power of myth, tradition, and the human desire for prosperity and well-being. Even today, new Inari shrines are still being constructed, and existing shrines are meticulously renovated, underscoring the continued importance of this deity in the spiritual life of Japan. The deity's capacity to adapt to modern life, while simultaneously retaining its core associations with agriculture and prosperity, guarantees its continued relevance in the future. The multifaceted nature of Inari, represented in the various forms, epithets, and manifestations, also contributes to its enduring appeal, as individuals can connect with the deity in diverse ways, tailored to their personal needs, beliefs, and aspirations. The ubiquitous presence of fox statues, red *torii* gates, and other iconic symbols of Inari serve as constant reminders of the deity's pervasive presence and profound influence within Japanese society. The unwavering veneration of Inari stands as a testament to the enduring power of myth and the crucial role of tradition in shaping cultural identity.

The cultural significance of Inari extends beyond religious practices and permeates various art forms, including literature, theater, visual arts, and contemporary media. Inari and the fox messengers frequently appear in traditional folktales, plays (such as Noh and Kabuki), and paintings, often serving as potent symbols of good fortune, prosperity, protection, and even transformation. The image of the fox has been widely adopted in popular culture, appearing in anime, manga, video games, and other forms of entertainment, further solidifying Inari's place in the collective consciousness of Japan. The artistic representations of Inari often reflect the deity's multifaceted nature, depicting Inari in a range of forms and guises, from benevolent protector to cunning trickster, depending on the specific narrative context. These artistic interpretations contribute to the ongoing evolution of Inari's mythology, ensuring that the deity remains relevant and engaging for contemporary audiences. The strategic use of symbolism in Inari's iconography, such as the rice bale, the sickle, the key, and the jewel, further enhances the artistic appeal and cultural significance of the deity. These symbols serve as visual reminders of Inari's associations with agriculture, prosperity, and the power to unlock hidden treasures and potential. The enduring presence of Inari in Japanese art and culture stands as a testament to the deity's profound impact on the nation's identity and creative expression.

In conclusion, Inari is a multifaceted and deeply ingrained deity within the Japanese Shinto tradition. From the humble origins as a *kami* of rice to the embodiment of prosperity, commerce, and even certain aspects of household safety and wellbeing, Inari's influence is undeniable and pervasive. The ubiquitous presence of the fox messengers, the vibrant red *torii* gates that mark sacred spaces, and the continued devotion of countless individuals across Japan all serve as testaments to the enduring power and relevance of Inari in the spiritual and cultural landscape of the nation. The deity’s remarkable ability to adapt and evolve, while simultaneously retaining its core associations with agriculture and abundance, ensures its continued importance in the lives of the Japanese people. Inari remains a potent symbol of hope, prosperity, and the enduring connection between humanity and the natural world, a connection that continues to shape the cultural and spiritual identity of Japan.

sources.title: Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters)
sources.author: Ō no Yasumaro (compiler)
sources.text: The Kojiki provides early accounts of Japanese mythology and mentions deities associated with food and agriculture, relevant to Inari's role, particularly the deity Ukanomitama.

sources.title: Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan)
sources.author: Various compilers
sources.text: Similar to the Kojiki, the Nihon Shoki offers valuable insights into the early development of Japanese religious beliefs and agricultural practices, and also mentions Ukanomitama.

sources.title: Inari Jinja: Of Rice, Foxes, and Kami
sources.author: Karen A. Smyers
sources.text: A comprehensive academic study of Inari worship, exploring its historical development, iconography, and cultural significance. Smyers delves into the syncretic nature of Inari worship and the role of foxes in the deity's cult.

sources.title: A Popular Dictionary of Shinto
sources.author: Brian Bocking
sources.text: Provides concise definitions and explanations of key terms and concepts related to Shinto, including Inari and related deities.

sources.title: Shinto: The Kami Way
sources.author: Motoori Norinaga
sources.text: An influential work on Shinto thought and practice, offering insights into the nature of kami and the significance of ritual purity.

icon:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 64 64">
  <circle cx="32" cy="32" r="28.8" fill="#8b7fff" opacity="0.2"/>
  <path d="M 32 12.8 L 44.8 51.2 L 19.2 51.2 Z" fill="#8b7fff"/>
  <circle cx="32" cy="32" r="9.6" fill="#9370DB"/>
  <text x="32" y="57.6" text-anchor="middle" font-size="19.2" fill="#8b7fff">⚡</text>
</svg>
description:
Izanami-no-Mikoto (伊弉冉尊/伊邪那美命), often referred to simply as Izanami, holds a paramount position within Japanese mythology and Shinto cosmology. Her name, translating to "She Who Invites," alludes to her active and vital role in the genesis of the world and the subsequent unfolding of events that shaped the Japanese archipelago, its cultural identity, and its extensive pantheon of deities, the *kami*. Izanami's narrative is primarily chronicled in the *Kojiki* (Records of Ancient Matters), a text compiled in 712 CE, and the *Nihon Shoki* (Chronicles of Japan), completed in 720 CE. While these texts are not considered strictly historical accounts in the modern sense, they are indispensable sources for understanding the foundational beliefs, worldviews, and societal structures of early Japan. These texts portray Izanami as a primordial deity, inextricably linked to the core themes of creation, procreation, life, death, and the cyclical nature of existence itself.

According to Shinto mythology, Izanami's origin is traced back to the very genesis of the cosmos. In the beginning, before the existence of tangible land or defined forms, the world existed as a chaotic, amorphous mass, often described as a primordial soup. From this formless void emerged several generations of *kami*, each playing a role in the gradual ordering of the universe. Izanagi-no-Mikoto (He Who Invites) and Izanami were the last of these generations of primordial *kami*. They were entrusted with the monumental task of solidifying and bringing order to the formless chaos, transforming it into a structured and habitable world. This divine mandate positioned them as central figures in the Shinto creation narrative.

To accomplish their task, Izanagi and Izanami were bestowed with a sacred jeweled spear, the *Ame-no-Nuboko* (Heavenly Jeweled Spear). This spear served as a conduit for their divine power and intention. They were instructed to descend to the floating bridge of heaven and use the spear to stir the chaotic waters below. As they lifted the spear, drops of brine dripped from its tip and coalesced, solidifying to form the first island, known as Onogoro-shima (Self-Forming Island). This pivotal act marks the beginning of the physical creation of the Japanese archipelago, establishing Izanami and Izanagi as the progenitors of the land itself.

Upon the newly formed island of Onogoro-shima, Izanagi and Izanami constructed a palace, a dwelling place for themselves as divine beings. At the center of this palace, they erected a sacred pillar, the *Ame-no-Mihashira* (Heavenly Pillar), symbolizing the connection between the earthly realm and the heavens. They then embarked on a ritualistic dance around this pillar, circling it in opposite directions. Upon meeting, Izanami, according to some accounts, improperly initiated the marriage greeting. This ritualistic dance and subsequent union led to the procreation of the other islands that constitute the Japanese archipelago. This process of creation is a crucial element of the Shinto creation myth, emphasizing the importance of union, harmony, and the balance of opposing forces in bringing forth new life and form.

The order in which the islands were born is imbued with symbolic significance, reflecting a hierarchical understanding of the world inherent in Shinto cosmology. The larger, more strategically important islands, such as Awaji and Shikoku, were born first, followed by smaller, less significant ones. This hierarchical creation process underscores Izanami's role as a mother goddess, responsible for not only giving birth to the physical land but also establishing a structured and ordered world. The creation of the islands is not simply a physical act; it is a cosmogonic event that establishes the foundation for Japanese culture and identity.

Following the creation of the islands of Japan, Izanami and Izanagi proceeded to give birth to a multitude of *kami*, each representing different aspects of the natural world and human experience. These *kami* embodied various forces and phenomena, such as mountains, rivers, winds, trees, and even abstract concepts like justice and prosperity. This act of divine procreation populated the world with a diverse and interconnected network of spiritual entities, each playing a role in the maintenance and functioning of the cosmos. The birth of these *kami* further solidifies Izanami's role as a mother goddess and a fundamental force in the creation and ordering of the universe.

However, this process of creation was not without its inherent dangers and tragic consequences. Izanami's final act of creation, the birth of Kagutsuchi (also known as Homusubi), the *kami* of fire, resulted in her agonizing death. The intense heat emanating from Kagutsuchi burned Izanami severely, leading to her descent into Yomi, the land of the dead, also known as the underworld. This catastrophic event marks a pivotal turning point in the mythology, introducing the concept of death and the separation of the divine and mortal realms. It also establishes Yomi as a distinct and separate domain, ruled by Izanami in her transformed state.

Izanagi, overwhelmed with profound grief and inconsolable rage at the loss of his beloved wife, slew Kagutsuchi in retribution for his mother's death. From the blood of Kagutsuchi, even more *kami* were born, further illustrating the intricate interconnectedness of life, death, and creation within Shinto belief. This act of violence, while born of grief, paradoxically contributes to the ongoing process of creation, highlighting the cyclical nature of existence and the transformative power of death. However, Izanagi could not reconcile himself to Izanami's irreversible death and made the fateful decision to venture into Yomi in a desperate attempt to retrieve her. This perilous journey to the underworld is a pivotal episode in the mythology, exploring the themes of love, loss, grief, and the inviolable boundaries between the world of the living and the realm of the dead.

Izanagi's descent into Yomi is fraught with peril and symbolic challenges. The land of the dead is depicted as a dark, decaying, and desolate place, a stark contrast to the vibrant and life-affirming world of the living. It is a realm of shadows and corruption, far removed from the familiar order and beauty of the earthly realm. When Izanagi finally locates Izanami within the depths of Yomi, he discovers that she is no longer the radiant and beautiful goddess he remembers from their life together. Her form has been transformed by the decay of death, and she is surrounded by malevolent spirits and demons. She warns him not to look upon her in her current state, acknowledging the profound transformation she has undergone and the inherent danger of witnessing the corruption of death. However, Izanagi, driven by his unwavering love and intense longing, disobeys her warning and violates the boundaries of the underworld.

Driven by his love and his inability to accept her death, Izanagi ignites a tooth from his comb, transforming it into a torch to illuminate the darkness of Yomi. In the flickering light, he beholds Izanami in her horrifying, decaying form. This shocking sight fills him with overwhelming terror, disgust, and a profound sense of violation. The image of Izanami's corrupted form shatters his idealized memory of her and forces him to confront the harsh reality of death and decay. Overcome with fear and revulsion, Izanagi flees from Yomi in a desperate attempt to escape the horrors he has witnessed.

Izanami, deeply enraged by Izanagi's betrayal of her trust and his intrusion into the sacred and inviolable realm of the dead, orders the *Shikome* (hideous women) to pursue him relentlessly. These demonic figures, embodying the corruption and decay of Yomi, are tasked with bringing Izanagi back to the underworld. Izanagi, utilizing his cunning, divine powers, and the resources at his disposal, attempts to delay and evade his pursuers. He throws objects behind him, such as his headdress, which transforms into grapes, and his comb, which transforms into bamboo shoots, providing temporary distractions for the *Shikome*. He eventually reaches the entrance to Yomi, the boundary between the world of the living and the realm of the dead, where he places a massive boulder, known as the Rock Door of Yomi, to permanently seal the passage and prevent further incursions.

Izanami, now irrevocably the queen of the underworld, stands on the other side of the boulder, confronting Izanagi across the divide. In a moment of profound separation and resentment, she declares that she will kill one thousand people every day, asserting her dominion over death and her determination to claim lives from the world of the living. Izanagi, in response, retorts that he will ensure that fifteen hundred people are born every day, counteracting her deathly decree and reaffirming the power of life and procreation. This exchange establishes the fundamental cycle of life and death, a constant interplay between creation and destruction. It solidifies Izanami's definitive role as the queen of the underworld, forever bound to the realm of death and decay, and underscores the inevitable presence of death as an intrinsic part of the human experience.

After his harrowing and traumatic experience in Yomi, Izanagi undergoes a rigorous purification ritual to cleanse himself of the defilement and spiritual contamination he incurred through his contact with death. As he washes himself in a clear stream, new *kami* are miraculously born from his body. Amaterasu (the sun goddess), the most revered of all the *kami*, is born from his left eye, representing light, order, and the source of life. Tsukuyomi (the moon god) is born from his right eye, embodying the night, the passage of time, and the reflective nature of the moon. Susanoo (the storm god) is born from his nose, representing chaos, destruction, and the untamed forces of nature. These three deities are considered the most important and powerful of all the *kami* and are collectively known as the Three Precious Children. Their birth marks the completion of the initial stage of the creation myth and establishes the foundation for the Japanese pantheon, setting the stage for the subsequent interactions and narratives that shape the mythology.

Izanami's role in Japanese mythology is multifaceted, complex, and deeply symbolic. She is revered as a mother goddess, responsible for the creation of the land, the birth of numerous *kami*, and the establishment of order in the cosmos. Simultaneously, she is feared as the queen of the underworld, associated with death, decay, and the darker, more mysterious aspects of existence. Her story explores the fundamental and enduring themes of creation, life, death, love, loss, grief, and the cyclical nature of existence itself. Her relationship with Izanagi highlights the critical importance of balance and harmony in the cosmos, demonstrating the interplay between opposing forces. The conflict between them establishes the inviolable boundaries between the living and the dead and underscores the inevitability of death as an integral and unavoidable part of life's journey.

The symbolism associated with Izanami is rich, layered, and deeply resonant. As a mother goddess, she embodies fertility, creation, and the life-giving forces of nature. Her descent into Yomi symbolizes the transition from life to death, the mysteries of the afterlife, and the unknown realms beyond human comprehension. The boulder that seals the entrance to Yomi represents the separation between the living and the dead, the boundaries that must be respected, and the consequences of violating those boundaries. Her declaration to kill one thousand people every day reflects the power, inevitability, and pervasive presence of death in the world. Conversely, Izanagi's response to ensure the birth of fifteen hundred people every day symbolizes the resilience of life, its capacity to overcome death, and the ongoing cycle of renewal and regeneration.

Culturally, Izanami holds a significant and enduring place in Shinto beliefs, rituals, and practices. She is frequently venerated in shrines and temples throughout Japan, particularly in those dedicated to fertility, childbirth, safe delivery, and the protection of the deceased. Her story is retold and re-enacted in festivals, ceremonies, and theatrical performances, serving as a constant reminder of the importance of respecting the balance between life and death, honoring the ancestors, and acknowledging the cyclical nature of existence. Her legacy continues to influence Japanese art, literature, and popular culture, where she is often depicted as a powerful, enigmatic, and complex figure, embodying both the creative and destructive forces of the universe.

In conclusion, Izanami-no-Mikoto is a foundational figure in Japanese mythology and a central deity in the Shinto pantheon. Her role as the mother of creation and the queen of the underworld underscores the cyclical nature of existence, the interconnectedness of life and death, and the profound mysteries that lie at the heart of human experience. Her story, as recounted in the *Kojiki* and the *Nihon Shoki*, provides invaluable insights into the early Japanese worldview, the enduring significance of Shinto beliefs, and the ongoing dialogue between humanity and the divine. Her legacy continues to resonate deeply within Japanese culture, reminding us of the profound mysteries of creation, the inevitability of death, and the enduring power and influence of the *kami*.

sources.title: Kojiki (Records of Ancient Matters)
sources.author: Ō no Yasumaro (compiler)
sources.text: The Kojiki is the oldest extant chronicle of Japan. It details the mythology, legends, and history of Japan, starting with the creation myth involving Izanagi and Izanami. It's crucial for understanding Izanami's role in the creation of the islands and the birth of the kami.

sources.title: Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan)
sources.author: Various compilers
sources.text: The Nihon Shoki is the second oldest book of classical Japanese history. It presents a more detailed and sometimes differing account of the myths and legends found in the Kojiki, including the story of Izanami's death and her role as the ruler of Yomi.

sources.title: A Popular Dictionary of Shinto
sources.author: Toshio Murakami
sources.text: Provides detailed information on Shinto deities and concepts, including Izanami's role in the Shinto pantheon and her connection to creation and death. Offers insights into the theological significance of Izanami's story.

sources.title: Shinto: The Kami Way
sources.author: Joseph Cali and John Dougill
sources.text: A comprehensive overview of Shinto beliefs and practices, including a discussion of the major kami such as Izanami and her impact on Japanese culture.

sources.title: Japanese Mythology A to Z
sources.author: Jeremy Roberts
sources.text: A useful reference guide to the key figures, places, and concepts in Japanese mythology, with a detailed entry on Izanami.
